title: Breakup applehv machine funcs
body:
The functions for AppleHV's VM interface implementation (machine.go) had quite large functions. Pulls out some code that could be moved to its own function for easier readability.

[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

<!--

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

comments.body: Nice work, just one more issue

> Also squash your commits.

@rhatdan, I asked @jakecorrenti to split such refactors into smaller.  It's good practice and makes reviews so much easier.
Sure if they are done like @mtrmac does them.  But if one PR changes a line of code, and the next PR changes the same line of code, then this does not help.

(a wild @mtrmac appears…)

From a one-minute spot check, this in fact looks pretty similar to how I approach refactors. And changing one line several times is not something I try to avoid.

In refactors, my basic approach is to make every commit so trivial that that it can be easily reproduced just from the commit message. That both reassures reviewers, and it makes rebasing easy — any merge conflict can be resolved by either processing the conflict, or just by throwing the previous version away and doing it again.

> In refactors, my basic approach is to make every commit so trivial that that it can be easily reproduced just from the commit message. That both reassures reviewers, and it makes rebasing easy — any merge conflict can be resolved by either processing the conflict, or just by throwing the previous version away and doing it again.

That's why I can review @mtrmac's PRs even after a long night in the pub :^)

But it's also a skill worth practicing - it's best practice. Unfortunately, GitHub isn't effective in highlighting commits so I feel this skill is at a collective decline.

With the commit in it's current form, it's barely possible to understand what was done and why.  There are absolutely not tests for HV at the moment.  Bisecting will not be fun...
